content:
Statistical **subset selection** for regression predates modern ML: **stepwise regression** (forward and backward) appeared in econometrics and biostatistics mid-20th century as computers made repeated model fitting feasible.

**Akaike (AIC)** and **Schwarz (BIC)** information criteria gave principled scoring of subsets by trading fit against complexity—early wrapper-style model comparison without exhaustive search.

**Lasso (L1)** was popularized by **Tibshirani (1996)** as a shrinkage method that also performs variable selection via sparse coefficients, linking regularization theory to subset choice.

**Guyon et al.** formalized **Recursive Feature Elimination** with SVMs in the early 2000s, showing wrapper loops could reach compact gene subsets in bioinformatics.

Information-theoretic filters such as **mutual information** gained traction as non-linear alternatives to correlation for ranking features in classification.

Today, sklearn unifies filters (`SelectKBest`), wrappers (`RFE`, `RFECV`), and embedded paths (`LassoCV`, tree importances) in pipelines—the same conceptual families, scaled to high-dimensional tabular and text problems in classical ML and as a precursor step before deep representation learning.

timeline.label: Stepwise selection
timeline.description: Forward/backward heuristics search regression subsets using incremental F-tests or score changes.

timeline.label: Lasso sparsity
timeline.description: L1 penalty yields exact zeros, embedding selection in convex linear model training.

timeline.label: RFE and MI filters
timeline.description: Wrapper elimination with SVMs and MI-based filters address high-dimensional bioinformatics and text.

timeline.label: Pipeline integration
timeline.description: Libraries compose selection with CV and leakage-safe preprocessing for production ML workflows.
